name: Nirvanamed.pt: WordPress site operations
description: WordPress-based clinic site tree with PHP 8.3 compatibility work and disciplined operations—change control, least exposure of credentials, and a clear separation between code, content, and secrets.
longDescription: WordPress deployment layout with core structure, themes and plugins under wp-content, and supporting tooling, plus technical notes for PHP 8.3 alignment, layout debugging, and local development. The focus is long-running site maintenance rather than a greenfield SPA: traceable fixes, verification notes, and repeatable workflows for the team running the site. Architecture is treated as an attack-surface map—wp-content, plugins, and uploads are reviewed as part of routine hygiene; configuration that could leak DB or SMTP credentials never lives in public trees.
features: Versioned site tree suitable for staged updates and audits
Technical write-ups for compatibility and layout verification
Separation between packaged assets, working directories, and host-only secrets
Operational documentation alongside the PHP application
Checklist-oriented mindset: updates, backups, and rollback paths documented where relevant
role: Maintenance, PHP upgrades, and deployment hygiene
highlights: Traceable change history for a live WordPress property—operations as engineering
PHP 8.3 migration and verification material captured in-repo
Practical packaging patterns for backups and hand-offs without baking secrets into artifacts
Security-aware maintenance: themes/plugins treated as first-class supply-chain and exposure risk
challenges: Long-lived WordPress installs accumulate theme/plugin drift and hosting-specific constraints; upgrades must be verifiable without exposing internal credentials in docs.
solutions: Keep operational notes and verification artifacts in version control while storing secrets only in server environment configuration; prefer documented checklists over ad-hoc production edits.
security: Credentials and connection strings belong only in host configuration, not in public documentation or VCS
Treat wp-content and custom modules as part of the attack surface—updates and reviews are first-class
Prefer role-based admin accounts, strong authentication, and least-privilege DB users on production hosts
Backups and exports reviewed for accidental inclusion of .env or private keys before sharing
